section.title: Boasting About Tomorrow
section.sectionRationale: James turns from worldliness in general to one of its most respectable forms: confident planning that leaves God out. He addresses the traveling merchant who maps out a year of profit as if tomorrow were his to command, exposes the arrogance of such self-assured boasting, and calls for a life lived under the Lord's will. He closes with a sharp reminder that knowing the good and not doing it is itself sin.
section.thesis: To plan our lives as though the future were ours is arrogant boasting, for life is a vanishing mist; humble living says, if the Lord wills, and refuses the sin of knowing the right and failing to do it.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the presumptuous planner rebuked and taught to say if the Lord wills; and the closing principle that knowing good and not doing it is sin.

verses.text: Come now, you who say, “Today or tomorrow we will go to this or that city, spend a year there, {{emporeuomai|carry on business}}, and make a profit.”
You do not even know what will happen tomorrow! What is your life? You are a {{atmis|mist}} that appears for a little while and then vanishes.
Instead, you ought to say, “If the Lord is willing, we will live and do this or that.”
As it is, you boast in your {{alazoneia|proud intentions}}. All such boasting is evil.
Anyone, then, who knows the right thing to do, yet fails to do it, is guilty of sin.

emporeuomai.gloss: To travel for trade, to carry on business, the root of the word emporium. James pictures the confident merchant planning a year of buying and selling to turn a profit. The problem is not commerce itself but the godless self-assurance with which the plan is made.

atmis.gloss: A vapor or mist, a puff of steam that appears briefly and is gone. James uses it for the brevity and fragility of human life, echoing the vapor of Ecclesiastes and the fleeting breath of the Psalms. The image demolishes the illusion that we command our own tomorrows.

alazoneia.gloss: Boasting, arrogance, pretentious self-confidence. It names the proud presumption of planning as though the future were ours to guarantee. James calls all such boasting evil, for it usurps a sovereignty that belongs to God alone, who holds every tomorrow in His hand.

basic.context: James takes on the successful planner. Picture a merchant saying, tomorrow we will go to that city, spend a year, do business, and make a profit. Sounds sensible. But James says, you do not even know what tomorrow holds. Your life is a mist, here for a moment and gone. The arrogance is not in planning but in planning as if you were in charge of the future. Instead, he says, live saying, if the Lord wills, we will live and do this or that. To leave God out and boast in your own plans is evil. And he ends with a jab that applies to everyone: if you know the right thing to do and do not do it, that is sin.
basic.hermeneutics: This is prescriptive wisdom, correcting an attitude rather than forbidding an activity. James is not against making plans; he is against the practical atheism of planning as though God were irrelevant and the future guaranteed. The phrase if the Lord wills is meant to reshape the heart's posture, not become a superstitious formula. Verse 17 broadens the point: sin is not only doing wrong but failing to do the good we know we ought to do.
basic.originalLanguages: The merchant plans to emporeuomai, to trade and profit. But life is an atmis, a mist or vapor, that vanishes. To plan without God is alazoneia, arrogant boasting, which James calls evil. The words move from confident commerce to the fragility of life to the pride that forgets its Maker.
basic.typology: The vanishing mist echoes the fleeting vapor of Ecclesiastes and the grass that withers in the Psalms and Isaiah, a theme fulfilled in the contrast with the eternal word and eternal life found in Christ. The call to live under God's will reflects the perfect submission of Jesus, who did only the Father's will and prayed not my will but yours.
basic.christ: Jesus lived the very posture James commands, always saying in effect if the Father wills, submitting even in Gethsemane, not my will but Yours be done. He is the eternal word that abides while our mist-like lives pass, and in Him our fleeting days are taken up into everlasting life. He also fulfilled all righteousness, never knowing the good and failing to do it, the sin verse 17 names.
basic.god: God is the sovereign Lord of every tomorrow, in whose will our living and doing are held. He is not an optional add-on to our plans but the One on whom our next breath depends. He is also the righteous Judge who counts as sin not only the evil we do but the good we leave undone. To live well is to live in humble reference to His will.
basic.crossReferences: Jesus told a parable of the rich fool who planned for years and died that night, Luke 12:16-21. Proverbs 27:1 says do not boast about tomorrow. Psalm 39:5 calls each life a mere breath. And Paul often qualified plans with if the Lord wills, 1 Corinthians 4:19.
basic.oneStory: Scripture keeps puncturing human pretension to control the future, from the rich fool to the withering grass, and keeps pointing to the God who holds all time and the Christ who submitted wholly to the Father's will. James applies this to everyday planning: our mist-like lives belong to the Lord, so we live saying if He wills, and we do the good we know. This humility is the wisdom from above at work, and it looks to the eternal life secured in Christ.

context.literary: This paragraph continues the assault on pride begun in verse 6 and parallels the warning to the rich that opens chapter 5, both introduced by the same come now. It targets a specific expression of self-sufficiency, presumptuous planning, and ends with a general maxim, verse 17, that rounds off the section on pride and worldliness.
context.historical: James addresses a mobile, commercial world in which Jewish and Christian merchants traveled between cities to trade. Confident business planning was a mark of the successful, and it easily bred a self-reliance that left God out. The mist image would resonate in a culture acquainted with the sudden reversals of travel, trade, and mortality.
context.canonical: The passage stands in the wisdom tradition's realism about human frailty, echoing Proverbs, the Psalms, and Ecclesiastes on the brevity of life, and it reflects Jesus' own teaching in the parable of the rich fool. Its closing definition of sin as omission complements the Bible's fuller account of sin as both commission and neglect of known duty.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To rebuke the arrogance of planning life as though the future were ours, to teach humble dependence expressed in living under the Lord's will, and to warn that knowing the right thing and failing to do it is itself sin.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage prescribes a posture: say, if the Lord wills, rather than boast in self-made plans. The portrait of the merchant is an illustrative description used to convict. Verse 17 lays down an abiding principle that reaches beyond planning to every known good we neglect.

debates.question: Is James condemning planning itself?

views.label: No; he condemns godless presumption, not prudence
views.case: James does not forbid making plans or doing business, both assumed as normal. He condemns planning as though the future were guaranteed and God were irrelevant. The corrective, if the Lord wills, keeps planning under humble submission to God's sovereignty.

views.label: He targets the boastful spirit behind the plans
views.case: Others stress verse 16: the evil is boasting in proud intentions. The problem is the arrogant heart that credits itself with control, not the act of foresight. Wise, God-dependent planning is fully compatible with James.

debates.bearing: Both agree the sin is presumption, not prudence. Christians may and should plan, but always in conscious dependence on the Lord who holds tomorrow.

debates.question: What is the point of verse 17 on knowing good and not doing it?

views.label: It defines sins of omission
views.case: James broadens sin beyond wrongdoing to include failing to do the good we know we should. This is a sin of omission, and it presses home the whole letter's insistence that faith must act. Knowing the right and neglecting it is guilt, not neutrality.

views.label: It applies the planning lesson directly
views.case: Others read it as the immediate application: now that you know to live under God's will, failing to do so is sin. Either way it removes the excuse of ignorance and demands that knowledge become obedience.

debates.bearing: Whether taken broadly or as immediate application, the verse teaches that known good left undone is real sin, sealing the letter's call to be doers of the word.

study.originalLanguages: The scene is set by emporeuomai, to travel and trade for profit, the ordinary confidence of the merchant. James shatters the illusion with atmis, a mist or vapor that appears briefly and vanishes, his image for the whole of human life. The heart of the rebuke is alazoneia, arrogant boasting, the pretentious self-assurance of planning as if the future were ours; all such boasting, James says, is evil. The remedy is the humble refrain, if the Lord wills, and the closing maxim that known good left undone is sin, turning the whole passage from mere caution into a call to God-dependent obedience.

typology.fulfillment: The biblical theme of fleeting human life, answered by the eternal word and everlasting life in Christ, 1 Peter 1:24-25.
Fulfilled in Christ, who did only the Father's will and prayed not my will but Yours, Luke 22:42.

typology.type: The rich fool who plans and dies
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' parable warning against laying up treasure without regard to God, Luke 12:16-21.

study.christ: Jesus embodies the very humility James commands. He lived under the Father's will at every turn, and in Gethsemane He prayed not my will but Yours be done, the perfect if the Lord wills. He is the eternal word that abides while our mist-like lives pass away, and by union with Him our fleeting days are gathered into everlasting life. And He alone never knew the good and left it undone, fulfilling all righteousness, so that His obedience covers our neglect and empowers our doing.
study.god: God is the sovereign Lord of tomorrow, holding every future day in His hand, so that our living and our doing depend entirely on His will. He is no optional addition to our plans but the One on whom our next breath rests. He is also the righteous Judge who reckons as sin not only the wrong we commit but the good we know and fail to do. To honor Him is to plan, work, and live in humble, glad reference to His will.

crossReferences.note: The rich fool plans for many years and dies that night, the parable behind James's warning.
Do not boast about tomorrow, for you do not know what a day may bring.
Each person's life is a mere breath, the frailty James pictures as a mist.
Paul qualifies his plans with if the Lord is willing, modeling verse 15.
Not my will but Yours be done, Christ living the very posture James commands.

study.oneStory: Again and again Scripture deflates human pretension to control the future, from the rich fool to the grass that withers, and points instead to the God who holds all time and the Son who submitted wholly to the Father's will. James brings this home to daily planning: our mist-like lives are the Lord's, so we say if He wills and we do the good we know. Such humility is the heavenly wisdom in action, and it rests its hope not in tomorrow's profit but in the everlasting life secured in Christ.

quiz.q: What is wrong with the merchant's plan in verses 13 to 14?

options.text: He plans the future as if it were his to command, forgetting life is a fleeting mist.
options.note: Verses 13 to 14: the sin is presumption about a future only God holds.

options.text: He assumes he will live and profit though he cannot know tomorrow.
options.note: True to the text, describing the false certainty involved.

options.text: He is doing business at all, which James forbids.
options.note: James does not forbid commerce; he rebukes godless self-assurance.

options.text: He is too cautious and should take bigger risks.
options.note: The problem is arrogance, not caution.

quiz.q: How does James say we ought to speak about our plans?

options.text: If the Lord is willing, we will live and do this or that.
options.note: Verse 15: plans held humbly under God's sovereign will.

options.text: With humble dependence on God rather than proud self-assurance.
options.note: True to the heart of it, if less exact than the words given.

options.text: By making no plans at all and simply waiting.
options.note: James corrects the attitude, not the act of planning.

options.text: By confidently asserting our goals to guarantee success.
options.note: That is the boasting James calls evil.

quiz.q: What image does James use for human life in verse 14?

options.text: A mist that appears for a little while and then vanishes.
options.note: Verse 14: life is brief and fragile, like a vapor.

options.text: Something brief and fragile, here and quickly gone.
options.note: True to the sense, capturing the mist image in other words.

options.text: A solid structure we can build on for decades.
options.note: The opposite of the fleeting mist James describes.

options.text: An endless span we can plan without limit.
options.note: James stresses precisely that our time is short and uncertain.

quiz.q: What does James call boasting in one's own plans?

options.text: Arrogant boasting, which he says is evil.
options.note: Verse 16: all such proud self-confidence is evil.

options.text: Proud presumption that leaves God out of the future.
options.note: True to why it is evil, naming the underlying pride.

options.text: A harmless and even admirable confidence.
options.note: James judges it evil, not admirable.

options.text: The proper attitude for ambitious believers.
options.note: The opposite of what James commends.

quiz.q: What does verse 17 teach about sin?

options.text: Knowing the right thing to do and failing to do it is sin.
options.note: Verse 17: sins of omission are real guilt, not neutrality.

options.text: Sin includes neglecting the good we know we ought to do.
options.note: True restatement of the principle of omission.

options.text: Only actively doing wrong counts as sin.
options.note: James expands sin to include known good left undone.

options.text: Ignorance and neglect are always excusable.
options.note: The verse removes the excuse when the good is known.

questions: James pictures confident plans made without reference to God. Where do your own plans quietly assume you are in control of the future?
Your life is a mist. How does honestly facing the brevity of life change what you give your days to?
What would it look like, practically, to hold your plans this week with a genuine if the Lord wills rather than as a superstitious phrase?
Verse 17 says known good left undone is sin. Is there a good you clearly know you should do but keep postponing?
Jesus prayed not my will but Yours. In what decision right now do you most need to pray that same prayer?

translationNotes.note: Carry on business renders emporeuomai, to travel and trade for profit; the picture is an ordinary merchant venture, rebuked not for commerce but for godless presumption.
Mist renders atmis, a vapor or puff of steam, echoing the fleeting breath of the Psalms and the vapor of Ecclesiastes.
Proud intentions renders alazoneia, arrogant boasting; James judges all such self-confident bragging about the future to be evil.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for James 4:13-17. No em or en dashes occur in this movement.
